1:
"And the fifth angel sounded, and I saw a star fall from heaven unto the earth: and to him was given the key of the bottomless pit." The witnesses note the fallen star given the key of the bottomless pit — opening the fifth trumpet. On "the fifth angel sounded, and I saw a star fall from heaven unto the earth," the grammatical voices note the symbol. Barnes and Robertson note the vision: "the fifth angel sounded, and I saw a star (aster) fall (fallen) from heaven unto the earth" — at the fifth trumpet a star is seen fallen to earth; the star (described as "him") is a personal being, not a literal star, for to "him" a key is given.

On the identity of the star, interpreters divide. The grammatical-historical Barnes (with Gill, the historicist tradition) reads the star, in the application to history, as a fallen leader or the rise of a false religion — many historicists apply the whole fifth-trumpet vision to Mahomet and the Saracen invasions (the "star" Mahomet or the apostasy that opened the pit). The latin-fathers Bede reads the star as the great enemy (Satan, fallen like lightning, Luke 10:18) who opens the heart of his own by heretical doctrine; the locusts that follow he takes as heretics. Victorinus reads in the recapitulatory frame.

On "to him was given the key of the bottomless pit," the grammatical-historical note the permission: "to him was given (by divine permission) the key of the bottomless pit (the abyss)" — the fallen being is permitted to unlock the abyss; the key is given, not seized; even the powers of the pit are loosed only by God's allowance. The verse displays the fallen star given the key of the bottomless pit.

The witnesses agree the substance: at the fifth trumpet a fallen star (a personal being) is given the key to open the abyss, by divine permission (Barnes, Robertson, Bede). The identity of the star and the locusts that follow divides the interpretive schools: the historicist (a false religion/Mahomet, Barnes, Gill), the idealist (Satan opening the pit of heresy, Bede), the recapitulatory (Victorinus). The verse displays the abyss-key crux opened. The verse marks the trumpet: the fifth. The verse displays the star: fallen. The verse displays the key: of the pit. The verse displays the permission: given. The vision displays the fallen being loosing the abyss by God's leave. The verse displays the star: a person. The verse displays the pit: unlocked. The verse displays the key: granted. The fallen star given the key of the bottomless pit displays the opening of the fifth trumpet: a star fallen from heaven, to whom is given the key of the abyss — read by the historicists of a false religion or apostasy (Barnes, Gill), by the idealist of Satan opening the pit of heresy (Bede), the abyss loosed only by divine permission. The verse displays the opened pit, from which smoke arises (v.2).
2:
"And he opened the bottomless pit; and there arose a smoke out of the pit, as the smoke of a great furnace; and the sun and the air were darkened by reason of the smoke of the pit." The witnesses note the smoke from the abyss darkening the sun and air. On "he opened the bottomless pit; and there arose a smoke out of the pit, as the smoke of a great furnace," the grammatical voices note the rising smoke. Barnes and Robertson note the effect of unlocking the abyss: "he opened the bottomless pit (the abyss); and there arose a smoke out of the pit, as the smoke of a great furnace" — when the pit is opened, a vast volume of smoke pours out, like that of a great furnace; the dense, obscuring smoke of the abyss.

On "the smoke of a great furnace," the witnesses note the volume. Barnes and Ellicott note that the smoke is thick and abundant, as from a furnace; the figure of a great, darkening cloud arising from the depths; in the historicist reading, the rise and spread of a darkening error or invading power.

On "the sun and the air were darkened by reason of the smoke of the pit," the latin-fathers Bede and the grammatical-historical note the darkening: "the sun and the air were darkened by reason of the smoke" — the smoke obscures the sun and air; the figure of spiritual or political darkness spreading; Bede reads it of heresy obscuring the light of truth even for the lofty; the historicists, of the darkness brought by a false religion or invading host. The verse displays the smoke from the abyss darkening the sun and air.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the smoke — "he opened the bottomless pit; and there arose a smoke... as the smoke of a great furnace," a vast obscuring smoke pouring from the opened abyss (Barnes, Robertson); the smoke thick and abundant, a darkening cloud from the depths (Barnes, Ellicott); "the sun and the air were darkened," the smoke obscuring sun and air, the figure of spreading darkness — heresy (Bede) or a false power (historicists). The verse displays the darkening smoke. The verse marks the opening: the pit. The verse displays the smoke: as a furnace. The verse displays the darkening: sun and air. The verse displays the figure: spreading darkness. The smoke displays the abyss darkening the light. The verse displays the pit: opened. The verse displays the smoke: rising. The verse displays the sun: darkened. The smoke from the abyss darkening the sun and air displays the consequence of the opened pit: a great furnace-smoke arises and darkens the sun and the air — the figure of a darkening error or power spreading from the depths. The verse displays the darkened sky, from which the locusts emerge (v.3).
3:
"And there came out of the smoke locusts upon the earth: and unto them was given power, as the scorpions of the earth have power." The witnesses note the locusts from the smoke given scorpion power — the crux of the trumpet's meaning. On "there came out of the smoke locusts upon the earth," the grammatical voices note the swarm. Barnes and Robertson note the emergence: "there came out of the smoke locusts (akrides) upon the earth" — from the darkening smoke a swarm of locusts emerges; locusts, the proverbial symbol of a vast, devastating, devouring host (Joel 1-2; Exodus 10).

On the identity of the locusts, the interpretive schools divide. The grammatical-historical Barnes (with the Reformed Gill and the historicist tradition) reads the locusts as a symbol of an invading host — most historicists apply them to the Saracen/Arab armies of Mahomet, swarming out of Arabia (the locust the emblem of the Orient and of an all-devouring army). The latin-fathers Bede reads the locusts as heretics — men (v.4) who, like the dragon's members, sting with their mouths and carry poison in their tails (an idealist/spiritual reading). A futurist reading takes them as a literal, future demonic plague released from the abyss; a symbolic-idealist reading takes them as spiritual torments. Victorinus reads in the recapitulatory frame.

On "unto them was given power, as the scorpions of the earth have power," the grammatical-historical note the scorpion power: the locusts are given "power, as the scorpions of the earth have power" — not the ordinary power of locusts (to devour vegetation) but the stinging, tormenting power of scorpions; their hurt is the painful sting, not destruction of crops (v.4-5). The verse displays the locusts from the smoke given scorpion power.

The witnesses agree the substance: from the abyss-smoke a locust-swarm emerges, given the tormenting power of scorpions, to afflict men (Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Bede). But the locusts' identity divides the interpretive schools: the historicist (the Saracen/Mahometan armies, Barnes, Gill), the idealist (heretics or spiritual torments, Bede), the futurist (a literal demonic plague), the recapitulatory (Victorinus). The verse displays the locust crux. The verse marks the swarm: from the smoke. The verse displays the debate: Saracens, heretics, or demonic plague. The verse displays the power: as scorpions. The verse displays the hurt: tormenting sting. The locusts display the scorpion-stinging host loosed from the abyss. The verse displays the locusts: emerging. The verse displays the scorpions: their power. The verse displays the torment: their sting. The locusts from the smoke given scorpion power display the heart of the fifth-trumpet crux: a locust-swarm emerges from the abyss-smoke, armed with the tormenting sting of scorpions — read by the historicists as the Saracen armies of Mahomet (Barnes, Gill), by the idealist as heretics or spiritual torments (Bede), by the futurist as a literal demonic plague. The verse displays the scorpion-locusts, limited to torment the unsealed (vv.4-5).
4:
"And it was commanded them that they should not hurt the grass of the earth, neither any green thing, neither any tree; but only those men which have not the seal of God in their foreheads." The witnesses note the locusts restrained to hurt only the unsealed. On "it was commanded them that they should not hurt the grass of the earth, neither any green thing, neither any tree," the grammatical voices note the unusual restriction. Barnes and Robertson note the reversal of locust-nature: "it was commanded them that they should not hurt the grass of the earth, neither any green thing, neither any tree" — unlike natural locusts (which devour vegetation), these are forbidden to harm grass, green things, or trees; their target is not the vegetable world but men; the restriction marks them as no ordinary locusts.

On the restraint, the witnesses note the divine command. Barnes and Ellicott note that the locusts act under command ("it was commanded them") — they are restrained and directed; their power is bounded; they can hurt only what they are permitted to hurt; even the abyss-host is under God's control.

On "only those men which have not the seal of God in their foreheads," the latin-fathers Bede and the grammatical-historical note the spared sealed: the locusts hurt "only those men which have not the seal of God in their foreheads" — the sealed servants of God (7:3-4) are protected; only the unsealed, the ungodly, are tormented; Bede notes the locusts are sent to men, not grass, proving they signify men (heretics); the sealing of God's own shields them from the plague. The verse displays the locusts restrained to hurt only the unsealed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the restriction — "they should not hurt the grass... neither any green thing, neither any tree," the locusts forbidden to harm vegetation, their target men not crops (Barnes, Robertson); the locusts under command, restrained and bounded (Barnes, Ellicott); "only those men which have not the seal of God," the sealed protected, only the unsealed tormented (7:3-4, Bede, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the restrained plague. The verse marks the command: not the grass. The verse displays the target: men, not vegetation. The verse displays the spared: the sealed. The verse displays the afflicted: the unsealed. The restraint displays the plague bounded to the ungodly. The verse displays the grass: spared. The verse displays the seal: protecting. The verse displays the unsealed: hurt. The locusts restrained to hurt only the unsealed display the bounded, directed plague: forbidden to harm grass or trees, the locusts may torment only those who lack the seal of God — the sealed servants of God are shielded, only the ungodly afflicted. The verse displays the sealed and unsealed, the latter tormented five months (v.5).
5:
"And to them it was given that they should not kill them, but that they should be tormented five months: and their torment was as the torment of a scorpion, when he striketh a man." The witnesses note the locusts permitted to torment, not kill, for five months. On "to them it was given that they should not kill them, but that they should be tormented five months," the grammatical voices note the limited harm. Barnes and Robertson note the bounded plague: "to them it was given (permitted) that they should not kill them, but that they should be tormented (afflicted with pain) five months" — the locusts may not kill but only torment; their power is to inflict pain, not death; and the torment is limited to "five months" (the natural lifespan of locusts, or a defined, bounded period).

On "five months," the witnesses note the limited duration. Barnes and Ellicott note that "five months" denotes a limited, appointed time — whether the literal locust-season (about five months) or, symbolically, a defined period; in the historicist reading, a prophetic period; the torment is severe but bounded.

On "their torment was as the torment of a scorpion, when he striketh a man," the grammatical-historical note the scorpion-sting: the torment is "as the torment of a scorpion, when he striketh (stings) a man" — the pain is like a scorpion's sting, acute and agonizing but not fatal; the figure of intense, tormenting affliction. The verse displays the locusts permitted to torment, not kill, for five months.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the limited harm — "they should not kill them, but that they should be tormented five months," the locusts permitted to torment, not kill, for a bounded period (Barnes, Robertson); "five months" a limited appointed time, the locust-season or a defined period (Barnes, Ellicott); "as the torment of a scorpion, when he striketh a man," the acute, agonizing but non-fatal sting (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the tormenting plague. The verse marks the limit: not kill. The verse displays the torment: five months. The verse displays the figure: a scorpion's sting. The verse displays the bound: a defined period. The plague displays severe but limited, non-fatal torment. The verse displays the killing: forbidden. The verse displays the torment: five months. The verse displays the sting: scorpion's. The locusts permitted to torment, not kill, for five months display the bounded severity of the plague: they may not kill but only torment for five months, with pain like a scorpion's sting — severe, agonizing, but limited and non-fatal. The verse displays the scorpion-torment, so dreadful that men seek death (v.6).
6:
"And in those days shall men seek death, and shall not find it; and shall desire to die, and death shall flee from them." The brief verse notes that men will seek death but not find it. The grammatical voices note the desperation. Barnes and Robertson note the extremity of the torment: "in those days shall men seek death, and shall not find it; and shall desire to die, and death shall flee from them" — the torment is so unbearable that men long for death as a release, but death eludes them; the anguish drives them to seek to die, yet they cannot; the depth of the misery.

On "seek death, and shall not find it," the witnesses note the unrelieved suffering. Barnes and Ellicott note that the inability to find death heightens the horror — the sufferers cannot escape even by dying; the plague holds them in living torment from which death would be a mercy, but it is denied.

On "death shall flee from them," the grammatical-historical note the figure: "death shall flee from them" — death, normally feared, is now desired, yet it flees; the reversal marks the extremity of the woe; the torment is worse than death, yet death will not come. The verse displays that men will seek death but not find it.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the desperation — "men shall seek death, and shall not find it; and shall desire to die, and death shall flee from them," the torment so unbearable that men long for death but cannot find it (Barnes, Robertson); the inability to find death heightening the horror, no escape even by dying (Barnes, Ellicott); "death shall flee from them," death desired yet fleeing, the extremity of the woe (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the unrelieved torment. The verse marks the seeking: of death. The verse displays the failure: not found. The verse displays the desire: to die. The verse displays the flight: death flees. The desperation displays men longing for the death that eludes them. The verse displays the death: sought. The verse displays the dying: desired. The verse displays the fleeing: of death. That men will seek death but not find it displays the extremity of the plague's torment: the suffering is so dreadful that men seek death and long to die, yet death flees from them — a living anguish worse than death, from which they cannot escape. The verse displays the unrelieved woe, inflicted by the war-like locusts (vv.7-10).
7:
"And the shapes of the locusts were like unto horses prepared unto battle; and on their heads were as it were crowns like gold, and their faces were as the faces of men." The witnesses note the war-like appearance of the locusts. On "the shapes of the locusts were like unto horses prepared unto battle," the grammatical voices note the martial figure. Barnes and Robertson note the description (drawing on Joel 2:4): "the shapes of the locusts were like unto horses prepared unto battle" — the locusts appear like war-horses arrayed for battle; the figure of a disciplined, advancing army; in the historicist reading, the Arab cavalry.

On "on their heads were as it were crowns like gold," the witnesses note the crowns. Barnes and Ellicott note that "on their heads were as it were crowns like gold (something resembling golden crowns)" — the locusts wear what look like golden crowns; the symbol of victory, royalty, or conquest; the conquering, crowned host; some historicists see the turbans of the Arabs.

On "their faces were as the faces of men," the grammatical-historical note the human faces: "their faces were as the faces of men" — the locusts have human faces, marking intelligence and (in the symbolic reading) that they represent men, a reasoning host, not mere insects; the war-like, crowned, human-faced figure builds the picture of a formidable army. The verse displays the war-like appearance of the locusts.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the martial figure — "the shapes of the locusts were like unto horses prepared unto battle," the locusts like war-horses arrayed for battle (Joel 2:4), a disciplined army (Barnes, Robertson); "crowns like gold," golden-seeming crowns, the symbol of victory or conquest, the turbans of the Arabs to some (Barnes, Ellicott); "faces as the faces of men," human faces marking intelligence, representing men (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the army-locusts. The verse marks the shape: war-horses. The verse displays the crowns: like gold. The verse displays the faces: of men. The verse displays the figure: a conquering host. The description displays the locusts as a formidable crowned army. The verse displays the horses: arrayed. The verse displays the crowns: golden. The verse displays the faces: human. The war-like appearance of the locusts displays the figure of a formidable host: shaped like horses arrayed for battle, wearing golden-seeming crowns, with human faces — the picture of a conquering, intelligent army. The verse displays the army-locusts, with hair, teeth, and breastplates (vv.8-9).
8:
"And they had hair as the hair of women, and their teeth were as the teeth of lions." The brief verse notes the locusts' womanly hair and lions' teeth. The grammatical voices note the further features. Barnes and Robertson note the details: "they had hair as the hair of women, and their teeth were as the teeth of lions" — the locusts have long hair like women's and teeth like lions'; the figures combine to portray a fierce, formidable host.

On "hair as the hair of women," the witnesses note the long hair. Barnes and Ellicott note that "hair as the hair of women" denotes long, flowing hair; some historicists see the long hair or beards of the Arabs; the figure of a wild, untamed appearance; long hair contributing to the strange, terrible aspect.

On "their teeth were as the teeth of lions," the grammatical-historical note the devouring teeth: "their teeth were as the teeth of lions" — the lion's teeth (drawn from Joel 1:6) signify devouring strength and ferocity; the locusts are equipped to devour like lions; the figure of a fierce, ravenous power. The verse displays the locusts' womanly hair and lions' teeth.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the features — "they had hair as the hair of women, and their teeth were as the teeth of lions," long flowing hair and lion-like teeth (Barnes, Robertson); "hair as the hair of women" long hair, the Arabs' hair or beards to some, a wild appearance (Barnes, Ellicott); "teeth as the teeth of lions," devouring strength and ferocity (Joel 1:6, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fierce locusts. The verse marks the hair: as women's. The verse displays the teeth: as lions'. The verse displays the figure: fierce and devouring. The verse displays the aspect: terrible. The features display the locusts wild-haired and lion-toothed. The verse displays the hair: long. The verse displays the teeth: devouring. The verse displays the host: fierce. The locusts' womanly hair and lions' teeth display the terrible aspect of the host: long flowing hair and devouring lion's teeth — the figure of a wild, fierce, ravenous army. The verse displays the fierce locusts, armed with iron breastplates (v.9).
9:
"And they had breastplates, as it were breastplates of iron; and the sound of their wings was as the sound of chariots of many horses running to battle." The brief verse notes the locusts' iron breastplates and the thunder of their wings. The grammatical voices note the armor and noise. Barnes and Robertson note the further martial details: "they had breastplates, as it were breastplates of iron; and the sound of their wings was as the sound of chariots of many horses running to battle" — the locusts wear what seem iron breastplates (the symbol of invulnerability and military array), and their wings roar like the rush of many chariots into battle.

On "breastplates of iron," the witnesses note the armor. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "breastplates of iron" signify the hardness, invulnerability, and martial equipment of the host; they are armed for war, hard to wound; some historicists see the iron mail of the invading cavalry.

On "the sound of their wings was as the sound of chariots of many horses running to battle," the grammatical-historical note the terrifying noise: the roar of their wings is "as the sound of chariots of many horses running to battle" (Joel 2:5) — the overwhelming, terrifying noise of an advancing army; the din of war; the sound itself strikes terror. The verse displays the locusts' iron breastplates and the thunder of their wings.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the armor and noise — "breastplates, as it were breastplates of iron; and the sound of their wings was as the sound of chariots of many horses running to battle," the iron breastplates of invulnerability and the roar of war-chariots (Barnes, Robertson); "breastplates of iron" the hardness and martial array, the iron mail of cavalry to some (Barnes, Ellicott); "the sound of their wings... chariots of many horses," the terrifying din of an advancing army (Joel 2:5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the armed locusts. The verse marks the breastplates: of iron. The verse displays the wings: thundering. The verse displays the sound: war-chariots. The verse displays the terror: of the noise. The features display the locusts armored and thunderous in advance. The verse displays the iron: breastplates. The verse displays the wings: roaring. The verse displays the chariots: many. The locusts' iron breastplates and the thunder of their wings display the martial terror of the host: armed with iron breastplates and roaring like a charge of many war-chariots — the figure of an invulnerable, overwhelming army. The verse displays the armored locusts, whose scorpion tails hurt men (v.10).
10:
"And they had tails like unto scorpions, and there were stings in their tails: and their power was to hurt men five months." The brief verse notes the scorpion tails by which the locusts hurt men. The grammatical voices note the stinging power. Barnes and Robertson note the return to the scorpion-figure (v.3, 5): "they had tails like unto scorpions, and there were stings in their tails: and their power was to hurt men five months" — the locusts have scorpion-like tails with stings; their power to hurt resides in these tails; the affliction lasts the appointed five months.

On "tails like unto scorpions... stings in their tails," the witnesses note the weapon. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "stings in their tails" are the instrument of the torment; in the historicist reading, the stinging tail signifies the corrupting, tormenting power of the false religion left behind in its wake (the doctrine that stings and poisons); the lasting hurt is in the tail.

On "their power was to hurt men five months," the grammatical-historical note the bounded affliction: "their power was to hurt men five months" — the tormenting power is again limited to five months (v.5); the plague, severe as it is, is bounded in time; the repetition stresses the defined, limited duration. The verse displays the scorpion tails by which the locusts hurt men.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the stinging power — "they had tails like unto scorpions, and there were stings in their tails," the scorpion-tails the instrument of torment (Barnes, Robertson); the stinging tail the corrupting, tormenting power left in the wake, the lasting hurt (Barnes, Ellicott); "their power was to hurt men five months," the affliction again bounded to five months (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the stinging tails. The verse marks the tails: like scorpions. The verse displays the stings: the weapon. The verse displays the power: to hurt men. The verse displays the limit: five months. The feature displays the locusts tormenting by their stinging tails. The verse displays the tails: scorpion-like. The verse displays the stings: hurting. The verse displays the five months: bounded. The scorpion tails by which the locusts hurt men display the instrument of the plague: tails like scorpions, with stings, by which their power to hurt men lasts the appointed five months — the tormenting, corrupting power residing in the tail. The verse displays the stinging locusts, ruled by the king of the abyss (v.11).
11:
"And they had a king over them, which is the angel of the bottomless pit, whose name in the Hebrew tongue is Abaddon, but in the Greek tongue hath his name Apollyon." The witnesses note the king of the locusts, the angel of the abyss, named Destroyer. On "they had a king over them, which is the angel of the bottomless pit," the grammatical voices note the ruler. Barnes and Robertson note the king of the swarm: "they had a king over them, which is the angel (ruler) of the bottomless pit (the abyss)" — unlike natural locusts (which "have no king," Proverbs 30:27), these are ruled by a king — the angel/ruler of the abyss; the host has a directing head from the pit.

On the identity of the king, interpreters note the views. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "angel of the bottomless pit" is the destroying power that governs the host — taken by many as Satan or a satanic angel, the presiding spirit of destruction; in the historicist reading, the leader or false prophet at the head of the invading false religion.

On "whose name in the Hebrew tongue is Abaddon, but in the Greek tongue hath his name Apollyon," the grammatical-historical note the name Destroyer: the king's name is "Abaddon (Hebrew) ... Apollyon (Greek)" — both meaning "Destroyer" or "Destruction"; the name marks his character — he is the destroyer, the embodiment of ruin; the host he leads is a destroying power. The verse displays the king of the locusts, the angel of the abyss, named Destroyer.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ruler — "they had a king over them, which is the angel of the bottomless pit," the locusts ruled by the angel/ruler of the abyss, unlike natural locusts (Proverbs 30:27, Barnes, Robertson); the king the destroying power, taken as Satan or a satanic angel, or the leader of the false religion (Barnes, Ellicott); "Abaddon... Apollyon," both meaning Destroyer, marking his character (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the king Destroyer. The verse marks the king: of the locusts. The verse displays the angel: of the abyss. The verse displays the name: Abaddon, Apollyon. The verse displays the meaning: Destroyer. The king displays the destroying ruler of the abyss-host. The verse displays the king: ruling. The verse displays the abyss: his realm. The verse displays the name: Destroyer. The king of the locusts, the angel of the abyss, named Destroyer, displays the head of the plague: the host is ruled by the angel of the bottomless pit, named Abaddon in Hebrew and Apollyon in Greek — the Destroyer; the destroying power at the head of the abyss-host. The verse displays the Destroyer-king, closing the first woe (v.12).
12:
"One woe is past; and, behold, there come two woes more hereafter." The brief verse notes the passing of the first woe and the coming of two more. The grammatical voices note the structuring announcement. Barnes and Robertson note the transition: "one woe is past; and, behold, there come two woes more hereafter" — the fifth trumpet (the locust-plague) is the first of three "woe" trumpets (announced in 8:13); the first woe being past, two more woes (the sixth and seventh trumpets) are yet to come; the announcement marks the structure of the remaining trumpets.

On "one woe is past," the witnesses note the completed plague. Barnes and Ellicott note that "one woe is past" — the locust-torment (the first woe) has run its course; the announcement signals the end of the fifth-trumpet vision and prepares for the sixth.

On "there come two woes more hereafter," the grammatical-historical note the coming woes: "there come two woes more hereafter" — the sixth trumpet (the Euphratean horsemen, the second woe) and the seventh trumpet (the third woe) remain; the worst is not yet over; the announcement heightens expectation of the heavier judgments to come. The verse displays the passing of the first woe and the coming of two more.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the transition — "one woe is past; and, behold, there come two woes more hereafter," the first woe-trumpet past, two more to come (8:13, Barnes, Robertson); "one woe is past," the locust-torment run its course, ending the fifth-trumpet vision (Barnes, Ellicott); "two woes more hereafter," the sixth and seventh trumpets remaining, the heavier judgments to come (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the woe-structure. The verse marks the first woe: past. The verse displays the coming: two more. The verse displays the structure: three woes. The verse displays the expectation: heavier judgments. The announcement displays the first woe ended, two yet to come. The verse displays the woe: passed. The verse displays the woes: two more. The verse displays the coming: hereafter. The passing of the first woe and the coming of two more display the structuring marker between the trumpets: the first woe (the locusts) is past, but two woes more are yet to come — the sixth and seventh trumpets. The verse displays the coming woes, opening the sixth trumpet (v.13).
13:
"And the sixth angel sounded, and I heard a voice from the four horns of the golden altar which is before God," The brief verse notes the voice from the golden altar at the sixth trumpet. The grammatical voices note the source of the command. Barnes and Robertson note the opening of the sixth trumpet: "the sixth angel sounded, and I heard a voice from the four horns of the golden altar which is before God" — at the sixth trumpet a voice comes from the golden altar of incense before God; the command for the next judgment issues from the altar.

On "the four horns of the golden altar," the witnesses note the place. Barnes and Henry note that the "golden altar... before God" is the altar of incense (cf. 8:3), associated with the prayers of the saints; the voice from its horns suggests that the coming judgment is, in some sense, an answer to the prayers of God's people for justice (cf. 6:10).

On "a voice from the... altar," the puritan Henry notes the connection to prayer: the command for the sixth-trumpet judgment comes from the altar of incense, where the saints' prayers ascend — the judgment is bound up with the prayers of the persecuted church; God's avenging acts answer the cries of his people. The verse displays the voice from the golden altar at the sixth trumpet.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the source — "the sixth angel sounded, and I heard a voice from the four horns of the golden altar which is before God," the command for the next judgment from the golden altar (Barnes, Robertson); the "golden altar before God" the altar of incense (8:3), associated with the saints' prayers (Barnes, Henry); the judgment an answer to the prayers of the persecuted church (6:10, Henry). The verse displays the altar-voice. The verse marks the trumpet: the sixth. The verse displays the voice: from the altar. The verse displays the altar: golden, of incense. The verse displays the connection: the saints' prayers. The voice displays the judgment issuing from the altar of prayer. The verse displays the altar: golden. The verse displays the horns: four. The verse displays the voice: commanding. The voice from the golden altar at the sixth trumpet displays the opening of the second woe: a voice from the four horns of the golden altar before God commands the next judgment — the avenging act bound up with the prayers of the saints. The verse displays the altar-command, loosing the four angels of the Euphrates (v.14).
14:
"Saying to the sixth angel which had the trumpet, Loose the four angels which are bound in the great river Euphrates." The witnesses note the command to loose the four angels bound at the Euphrates. On "saying to the sixth angel which had the trumpet, Loose the four angels," the grammatical voices note the command. Barnes and Robertson note the directive: "saying to the sixth angel which had the trumpet, Loose (set free) the four angels" — the altar-voice commands the trumpet-angel to release four angels who had been bound; the loosing of restrained powers for judgment.

On "the four angels which are bound," interpreters note their nature. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "four angels... bound" are agents of judgment, held back until the appointed time; whether evil angels (instruments of destruction) or the directing powers behind the invading host; they had been restrained, and are now loosed; in the historicist reading, the powers behind the Turkish/Ottoman invasion long held in check.

On "the great river Euphrates," the latin-fathers Victorinus and the grammatical-historical note the location: the angels were bound "in the great river Euphrates" — the Euphrates, the great eastern boundary, associated with the powers of the East (Assyria, Babylon, Parthia); the historicists read the loosing of the Euphratean angels as the unleashing of the Turkish/Ottoman armies from the East upon Christendom; Victorinus reads the four angels as the four nations across the Euphrates, held until they come with Antichrist. The verse displays the command to loose the four angels bound at the Euphrates.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "Loose the four angels," the trumpet-angel commanded to release the four bound angels for judgment (Barnes, Robertson); the "four angels bound" agents of judgment held until the appointed time, evil angels or directing powers, the Ottoman powers to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); "the great river Euphrates," the eastern boundary, the powers of the East loosed, Victorinus reading the four nations beyond it (grammatical-historical, Victorinus). The verse displays the loosed angels. The verse marks the command: loose them. The verse displays the angels: four, bound. The verse displays the place: the Euphrates. The verse displays the reading: eastern powers loosed. The command displays the restrained judgment-powers released. The verse displays the loosing: commanded. The verse displays the angels: bound. The verse displays the Euphrates: the place. The command to loose the four angels bound at the Euphrates displays the unleashing of the second woe: the altar-voice bids the trumpet-angel release the four angels bound at the great river Euphrates — read by the historicists as the loosing of the Ottoman armies from the East, by Victorinus as the four nations beyond the river. The verse displays the loosed angels, prepared to slay a third of men (v.15).
15:
"And the four angels were loosed, which were prepared for an hour, and a day, and a month, and a year, for to slay the third part of men." The witnesses note the four angels loosed at the appointed time to slay a third of men. On "the four angels were loosed, which were prepared for an hour, and a day, and a month, and a year," the grammatical voices note the precise appointment. Barnes and Robertson note the exact timing: "the four angels were loosed, which were prepared for (held ready against) an hour, and a day, and a month, and a year" — the angels had been prepared for a precisely appointed time; the accumulation of time-units (hour, day, month, year) stresses that the moment of judgment was exactly fixed by God; nothing is left to chance.

On "prepared for an hour, and a day, and a month, and a year," interpreters note the period. Barnes and Ellicott note two readings: the phrase may simply mean that the angels were held ready for the exact appointed moment; or, in the historicist reading, the time-units form a prophetic period (computed by some as the duration of the Ottoman power); the precise appointment of the judgment is the main point.

On "for to slay the third part of men," the grammatical-historical note the lethal commission: the angels are loosed "for to slay the third part of men" — unlike the locusts (who only tormented), this second woe is deadly: a third part of men is to be slain; the judgment is more severe, bringing death; the "third part" marks a limited but vast destruction. The verse displays the four angels loosed at the appointed time to slay a third of men.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the appointment — "the four angels were loosed, which were prepared for an hour, and a day, and a month, and a year," the angels held ready for a precisely fixed time, the moment exactly appointed by God (Barnes, Robertson); the time-units stressing the exact appointment, or (historicist) forming a prophetic period (Barnes, Ellicott); "to slay the third part of men," the deadly commission, a third slain, more severe than the locusts (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the appointed slaughter. The verse marks the loosing: at the fixed time. The verse displays the preparation: hour, day, month, year. The verse displays the commission: slay a third. The verse displays the severity: deadly. The angels display the exactly-timed, lethal judgment. The verse displays the angels: loosed. The verse displays the time: appointed. The verse displays the third: slain. The four angels loosed at the appointed time to slay a third of men display the precision and severity of the second woe: the angels, prepared for an exactly fixed hour, day, month, and year, are loosed to slay a third part of men — a deadly judgment more severe than the tormenting locusts. The verse displays the slaying angels, leading a vast army of horsemen (v.16).
16:
"And the number of the army of the horsemen were two hundred thousand thousand: and I heard the number of them." The brief verse notes the vast number of the horsemen — two hundred million. The grammatical voices note the immense host. Barnes and Robertson note the number: "the number of the army of the horsemen were two hundred thousand thousand (two myriads of myriads, two hundred million): and I heard the number of them" — the cavalry host is numbered at two hundred million; an immense, almost innumerable army; John heard the number declared.

On "two hundred thousand thousand," the witnesses note the symbolic immensity. Barnes and Ellicott note that the number (literally "two myriads of myriads," 200,000,000) is so vast as to signify an overwhelming, countless host; whether taken as a definite symbolic number or as a figure for an uncountable multitude; the historicists see the vast armies of the East; the staggering size marks the terror of the judgment.

On "I heard the number of them," the grammatical-historical note the certainty: "I heard the number of them" — John heard the number declared, marking its definiteness in the vision; the immensity is not vague but specified; the host is beyond ordinary reckoning yet precisely numbered to God. The verse displays the vast number of the horsemen.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the host — "the number of the army of the horsemen were two hundred thousand thousand," two hundred million, an immense cavalry host (Barnes, Robertson); the number (two myriads of myriads) signifying an overwhelming, countless host, the vast eastern armies to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); "I heard the number of them," the number declared, marking its definiteness (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the immense army. The verse marks the number: two hundred million. The verse displays the host: the horsemen. The verse displays the immensity: overwhelming. The verse displays the hearing: declared. The number displays the countless cavalry of the second woe. The verse displays the army: vast. The verse displays the number: heard. The verse displays the horsemen: innumerable. The vast number of the horsemen displays the terror of the second woe: the cavalry host numbered two hundred million — an overwhelming, almost countless army, whose number John heard declared. The verse displays the immense host, whose horses breathe fire (v.17).
17:
"And thus I saw the horses in the vision, and them that sat on them, having breastplates of fire, and of jacinth, and brimstone: and the heads of the horses were as the heads of lions; and out of their mouths issued fire and smoke and brimstone." The witnesses note the fire-breathing horses with lions' heads. On "I saw the horses in the vision, and them that sat on them, having breastplates of fire, and of jacinth, and brimstone," the grammatical voices note the description. Barnes and Robertson note the appearance of horses and riders: "the horses... and them that sat on them, having breastplates of fire (fiery red), and of jacinth (hyacinth, dusky blue), and brimstone (sulphurous yellow)" — the riders wear breastplates of three colors — red, blue, and yellow — matching the fire, smoke, and brimstone that issue from the horses; the terrible, hellish array.

On "the heads of the horses were as the heads of lions," the witnesses note the ferocity. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the heads of the horses were as the heads of lions" — the horses have lion-like heads, the symbol of ferocity, strength, and destruction; the figure of an overwhelming, devouring power.

On "out of their mouths issued fire and smoke and brimstone," the grammatical-historical note the deadly breath: "out of their mouths issued fire and smoke and brimstone" — the horses breathe fire, smoke, and brimstone, the instruments of the slaughter (v.18); the historicists see in the fire and smoke the gunpowder and artillery of the Ottoman armies (cannon and firearms); the deadly, hellish weaponry of the host. The verse displays the fire-breathing horses with lions' heads.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the description — "the horses... and them that sat on them, having breastplates of fire, and of jacinth, and brimstone," the riders in red, blue, and yellow breastplates matching the deadly issue (Barnes, Robertson); "heads of the horses were as the heads of lions," lion-headed, the symbol of ferocity and destruction (Barnes, Ellicott); "out of their mouths issued fire and smoke and brimstone," the deadly breath, the gunpowder and artillery to historicists (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hellish cavalry. The verse marks the breastplates: fire, jacinth, brimstone. The verse displays the heads: as lions. The verse displays the mouths: fire, smoke, brimstone. The verse displays the reading: artillery (historicist). The horses display the fire-breathing, lion-headed host. The verse displays the breastplates: tri-colored. The verse displays the heads: lion-like. The verse displays the breath: deadly. The fire-breathing horses with lions' heads display the terrible array of the second woe: horses with lion-heads, their riders in fiery breastplates, breathing fire, smoke, and brimstone from their mouths — read by the historicists as the artillery and firearms of the invading host. The verse displays the deadly horses, killing a third of men (v.18).
18:
"By these three was the third part of men killed, by the fire, and by the smoke, and by the brimstone, which issued out of their mouths." The brief verse notes the third of men killed by the fire, smoke, and brimstone. The grammatical voices note the lethal effect. Barnes and Robertson note the slaughter: "by these three (plagues) was the third part of men killed, by the fire, and by the smoke, and by the brimstone, which issued out of their mouths" — the three plagues — fire, smoke, and brimstone — issuing from the horses' mouths slay a third part of men; the deadly commission (v.15) is carried out; the second woe brings death, not mere torment.

On "these three... plagues," the witnesses note the threefold instrument. Barnes and Ellicott note that "these three" (fire, smoke, brimstone) are named as distinct plagues — the instruments by which the third part is killed; the historicists see the destructive power of the invading armies (artillery and the devastation of war); the threefold deadly issue.

On "the third part of men killed," the grammatical-historical note the limited but vast destruction: "the third part of men killed" — a third of mankind (or of the relevant population) is slain; the judgment is severe and extensive, yet bounded to a third; the "third part" marks a partial, not total, destruction — a warning judgment, not the final end. The verse displays the third of men killed by the fire, smoke, and brimstone.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the slaughter — "by these three was the third part of men killed, by the fire, and by the smoke, and by the brimstone," the three plagues from the horses' mouths slaying a third (Barnes, Robertson); "these three" the distinct deadly instruments, the destruction of war to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); "the third part of men killed," a severe but bounded destruction, partial not total (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the deadly plagues. The verse marks the three: fire, smoke, brimstone. The verse displays the effect: a third killed. The verse displays the instrument: the horses' mouths. The verse displays the bound: a third. The plagues display a third of men slain by the threefold deadly issue. The verse displays the three: lethal. The verse displays the third: killed. The verse displays the brimstone: deadly. The third of men killed by the fire, smoke, and brimstone displays the deadly execution of the second woe: by the three plagues issuing from the horses' mouths a third of men is slain — the severe but bounded judgment, partial and warning, not the final end. The verse displays the slain third, the horses' power in mouth and tail (v.19).
19:
"For their power is in their mouth, and in their tails: for their tails were like unto serpents, and had heads, and with them they do hurt." The brief verse notes the horses' power in their mouths and serpent tails. The grammatical voices note the twofold weapon. Barnes and Robertson note the source of their power: "their power is in their mouth, and in their tails: for their tails were like unto serpents, and had heads, and with them they do hurt" — the horses harm both with their mouths (the fire, smoke, brimstone) and with their tails (which are like serpents, with heads, that wound); a twofold instrument of hurt.

On "their power is in their mouth," the witnesses note the deadly breath. Barnes and Ellicott note that the power "in their mouth" is the fire and brimstone already described (vv.17-18) — the killing breath; the front of the host slays.

On "in their tails: for their tails were like unto serpents," the grammatical-historical note the serpent tails: "their tails were like unto serpents, and had heads, and with them they do hurt" — the tails are serpent-like, with heads, able to bite and wound; the host harms not only in its frontal attack but in its rear; the historicists see a lasting, poisonous power left in the wake of the invasion (false doctrine or continuing harm); the twofold power makes the host wholly deadly. The verse displays the horses' power in their mouths and serpent tails.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the twofold weapon — "their power is in their mouth, and in their tails," the horses harming with both mouth and tail (Barnes, Robertson); "in their mouth" the killing fire and brimstone (vv.17-18, Barnes, Ellicott); "in their tails: for their tails were like unto serpents, and had heads," the serpent-tails that bite and wound, a lasting poisonous power to historicists (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the twofold hurt. The verse marks the power: mouth and tails. The verse displays the mouth: fire and brimstone. The verse displays the tails: serpent-like. The verse displays the heads: that wound. The power displays the host deadly front and rear. The verse displays the mouth: killing. The verse displays the tails: biting. The verse displays the serpents: wounding. The horses' power in their mouths and serpent tails displays the wholly deadly nature of the host: their power is in both their mouths (the killing fire and brimstone) and their tails (serpent-like, with heads, that wound) — deadly in front and behind. The verse displays the twofold-deadly host, before whom the survivors still refuse to repent (v.20).
20:
"And the rest of the men which were not killed by these plagues yet repented not of the works of their hands, that they should not worship devils, and idols of gold, and silver, and brass, and stone, and of wood: which neither can see, nor hear, nor walk:" The witnesses note the impenitence of the survivors who would not forsake their idolatry. On "the rest of the men which were not killed by these plagues yet repented not of the works of their hands," the grammatical voices note the hardened survivors. Barnes and Robertson note the tragic result: "the rest of the men which were not killed by these plagues yet repented not of the works of their hands" — those who survived the deadly judgment still did not repent; the plagues, severe as they were, did not bring them to repentance; the hardening of the impenitent under judgment.

On "that they should not worship devils, and idols of gold, and silver, and brass, and stone, and of wood," the witnesses note the persistent idolatry. Barnes and Gill note the specific sin: they would not repent so as to stop worshiping "devils (demons), and idols of gold, and silver, and brass, and stone, and of wood" — they clung to demon-worship and idolatry; the survivors persisted in the very sins the judgment was meant to punish; the historicists see the idolatry and superstition that the plagues failed to reform.

On "which neither can see, nor hear, nor walk," the grammatical-historical note the folly of idols: the idols "neither can see, nor hear, nor walk" — the standard prophetic mockery of idols as dead, powerless, senseless things (Psalm 115:4-7); the folly of worshiping what is lifeless; their persistence in such worship after judgment marks their hardness. The verse displays the impenitence of the survivors who would not forsake their idolatry.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the hardening — "the rest of the men which were not killed by these plagues yet repented not of the works of their hands," the survivors unrepentant despite the deadly judgment (Barnes, Robertson); "that they should not worship devils, and idols of gold, and silver, and brass, and stone, and of wood," their persistent demon-worship and idolatry (Barnes, Gill); "which neither can see, nor hear, nor walk," the prophetic mockery of dead, senseless idols (Psalm 115:4-7, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the impenitent survivors. The verse marks the survivors: not killed. The verse displays the impenitence: repented not. The verse displays the sin: idolatry, demon-worship. The verse displays the folly: senseless idols. The impenitence displays the survivors clinging to idolatry under judgment. The verse displays the rest: hardened. The verse displays the idols: worshiped. The verse displays the senseless: gods. The impenitence of the survivors who would not forsake their idolatry displays the tragic hardening under judgment: those not killed by the plagues still did not repent of their idolatry, continuing to worship demons and lifeless idols of gold, silver, brass, stone, and wood. The verse displays the unrepentant idolaters, persisting also in their other sins (v.21).
21:
"Neither repented they of their murders, nor of their sorceries, nor of their fornication, nor of their thefts." The brief verse notes the survivors' impenitence in murder, sorcery, fornication, and theft. The grammatical voices note the catalogue of unrepented sins. Barnes and Robertson note the further impenitence: "neither repented they of their murders, nor of their sorceries, nor of their fornication, nor of their thefts" — beyond their idolatry (v.20), the survivors did not repent of their other gross sins — murder, sorcery, fornication, and theft; the catalogue of moral corruption they persisted in.

On "their murders... sorceries... fornication... thefts," the witnesses note the four sins. Barnes and Gill note the list of vices: "murders" (violence and bloodshed), "sorceries" (pharmakeia — witchcraft, magic arts, perhaps drug-related occultism), "fornication" (sexual immorality), and "thefts" (dishonesty, robbery) — the catalogue of the works of the flesh; the survivors clung to all these despite the judgment.

On the impenitence, the grammatical-historical note the lesson: the repeated "neither repented they" stresses the hardness of the human heart under judgment — that even deadly plagues do not, of themselves, produce repentance; judgment alone does not soften the impenitent; the heart must be changed by grace, not merely terrified by wrath. The verse displays the survivors' impenitence in murder, sorcery, fornication, and theft.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the catalogue — "neither repented they of their murders, nor of their sorceries, nor of their fornication, nor of their thefts," the survivors unrepentant of their gross moral sins beyond idolatry (Barnes, Robertson); the four vices — murder, sorcery, fornication, theft — the works of the flesh persisted in (Barnes, Gill); the repeated "neither repented" stressing the hardness of the heart under judgment, judgment alone not producing repentance (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the unrepented sins. The verse marks the sins: murder, sorcery, fornication, theft. The verse displays the impenitence: repented not. The verse displays the corruption: the works of the flesh. The verse displays the lesson: judgment alone hardens. The catalogue displays the survivors clinging to their vices under judgment. The verse displays the murders: unrepented. The verse displays the sorceries: persisted. The verse displays the impenitence: complete. The survivors' impenitence in murder, sorcery, fornication, and theft displays the depth of the hardening under judgment: they did not repent even of their murders, sorceries, fornication, and thefts — the heart unsoftened by wrath, persisting in every sin. The verse displays the unrepentant world, closing the second woe and the sixth trumpet.
